name: Cruz de Motrico and GR 99 Loop
name_original: Subida a la Cruz de Motrico - por los Montes Obarenses — circular desde Ircio
description: This difficult 8.8-mile jogging route to the Cross of Motrico from Ircio gains 1720 feet through the scenic Montes Obarenses.
region_intro: Jogging around Miranda De Ebro offers diverse terrain, from the plains traversed by the Ebro River to the challenging slopes of the Montes Obarenses mountain range. The region features a mix of Mediterranean scrub forests, with holm oaks and boxwood, alongside humid areas with oaks, beeches, and pines. Its geological diversity, including clayey soils, limestones, and sandstones, contributes to varied running surfaces. This landscape provides opportunities for both leisurely runs along riverbanks and more strenuous trail running with significant elevation gains. Best jogging routes around Miranda De Ebro The most popular jogging route is Cruz de Motrico and GR 99 Loop, an 8.8 miles (14.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 57 minutes to complete. This route gains over 1700 feet through the scenic Montes Obarenses mountains. Another top favourite among local runners is Motrico Cross loop from Miranda de Ebro, a difficult 22.4 miles (36.0 km) path. This challenging route features significant elevation changes, ascending over 4800 feet through varied mountain terrain. Local runners also love the Miranda de Ebro Train Station loop from Miranda de Ebro, a 7.9 miles (12.8 km) trail leading through urban areas and along mostly paved surfaces, often completed in about 1 hour 19 minutes. Jogging around Miranda De Ebro is defined by the Ebro River's banks, the foothills of the Montes Obarenses, and dedicated urban parks like Antonio Machado Park. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy, paved paths to difficult mountain trails. The routes in Miranda De Ebro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 870 runners have used komoot to explore Miranda De Ebro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Miranda de Ebro?
A: There are over 80 running routes around Miranda de Ebro, catering to various fitness levels. You'll find a diverse selection, from easy paths to challenging mountain trails.

Q: Are there easy running routes suitable for beginners in Miranda de Ebro?
A: Yes, Miranda de Ebro offers several easy running routes perfect for beginners or those seeking a relaxed pace. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40014592" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Antonio Machado Park – Miranda de Ebro Train Station loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 6.2 km path that takes you through urban areas and the scenic Antonio Machado Park.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in the Miranda de Ebro area?
A: Many of the running trails in Miranda de Ebro are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31451842" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miranda de Ebro Train Station loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 12.8 km route through urban areas and along mostly paved surfaces.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running trails around Miranda de Ebro?
A: The terrain around Miranda de Ebro is quite varied. You can expect everything from flat, paved paths along the Ebro River and through urban parks to challenging trails with significant elevation gains in the Montes Obarenses mountain range. The landscape features Mediterranean scrub forests, holm oaks, boxwood, and even humid areas with oaks and pines, offering diverse running surfaces.

Q: Are there any running routes that pass by interesting landmarks or attractions?
A: Absolutely. While running, you can explore various points of interest. For example, some routes might offer views of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4199700" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cruz de Motrico&lt;/a&gt; peak or pass near historical sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3223361" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portilla Castle&lt;/a&gt;. The Ebro River itself is a prominent natural landmark, with paths along its banks.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Miranda de Ebro?
A: The running routes in Miranda de Ebro are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 20 reviews. Runners often praise the variety of terrain, from riverbank paths to mountain trails, and the opportunities to experience both urban and natural landscapes.

Q: Are there any running routes suitable for families or those looking for a more relaxed pace?
A: Yes, for families or those preferring a relaxed pace, routes that stick to urban parks or riverbanks are ideal. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40014592" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Antonio Machado Park – Miranda de Ebro Train Station loop&lt;/a&gt; is a good example, offering a moderate, mostly flat experience through a picturesque park.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Miranda de Ebro?
A: Many outdoor trails in the Miranda de Ebro region are generally dog-friendly, especially those in natural areas like the Montes Obarenses. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash and check for any specific local regulations or signage, particularly in urban parks or protected natural spaces.

Q: Are there opportunities for wildlife spotting while running in Miranda de Ebro?
A: Given the region's diverse ecosystems, including Mediterranean scrub forests and humid areas with oaks and pines, there are opportunities for wildlife spotting. The area falls between two major biogeographical regions, resulting in extraordinary biological diversity, so keep an eye out for local flora and fauna, especially on trails away from urban centers.

Q: What are some of the longer, more challenging running routes for experienced runners?
A: For experienced runners seeking a challenge, Miranda de Ebro offers demanding routes with significant elevation. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31465949" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Motrico Cross loop from Miranda de Ebro&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 36.0 km path with over 1400 meters of ascent through varied mountain terrain, perfect for pushing your limits.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Miranda de Ebro?
A: Miranda de Ebro offers good running conditions throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide pleasant temperatures for outdoor activities. Summers can be warm, making early morning or late evening runs preferable, especially on exposed trails. Winters are milder, but higher elevation routes might require appropriate gear.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the Ebro River?
A: Yes, the Ebro River flows directly through Miranda de Ebro, and its banks provide picturesque views and dedicated paths. Many routes incorporate sections along the river, allowing you to enjoy the water and surrounding landscape. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31509719" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Ruinas Romanas de Deóbriga&lt;/a&gt; is one such route that offers views of the river and surrounding plains.
tips: The monastery of Herrera, currently known as Yermo Camaldolense de Nuestra Señora de Herrera, is located in the Montes Obarenes, in the municipality of Herrera, on the municipal border of Miranda de Ebro.
At the foot you can see the Camaldolese Yermo de Nuestra Señora de Herrera, and Miranda de Ebro.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The trail typically starts from San Juan del Monte, which is a common access point for the Montes Obarenes. You'll find amenities like parking, a fountain, and informative panels there to help you begin your journey.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the way to the Cross of Motrico?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter varied terrain, including wide tracks and narrower, well-marked paths. The ascent to the summit, particularly between San Juan del Monte and the Cross, goes through dense forests. Be prepared for a 'pending and demanding' firebreak section which can be steep and potentially muddy after rain, making it the most challenging part of the route.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers beautiful scenery, including thick forests of holm oaks, strawberry trees, and boxwoods. You'll pass by the Monasterio de Herrera, ascend to Peña la Abeja, and reach the Cruz de Motrico itself. From the summit, you'll get expansive panoramic views of the Ebro valley and surrounding mountains.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: This trail is classified as 'difficult' for jogging and a 'moderate hike' requiring 'good fitness'. The steep and potentially muddy firebreak section makes it challenging. While many paths are accessible, it's not an easy, flat route, so it might be demanding for beginners or very young children.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to experience this route?
faqs.answer: The Montes Obarenes offer beautiful scenery year-round, but consider visiting during spring or autumn for more pleasant temperatures and vibrant foliage. Be aware that the challenging firebreak section can become particularly muddy after rain, so drier periods are generally preferable.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required for hiking in Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no entrance fees or specific permits required for hiking this trail within the Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil Natural Park. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning environmental protection and fire prevention.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While many natural areas in Spain are dog-friendly, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure their safety on challenging sections. Given the 'difficult' rating and varied terrain, ensure your dog is fit enough for the climb.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several other paths. You'll find yourself on parts of the Camino natural de Ebro - Tramo central, Cazadores, Camino de la Loma, Camino del Portillo -Herrera, Camino de Los Corrales a Villalba, Camino del Colomo, and Carretera Miranda-Haro.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect from the Cross of Motrico?
faqs.answer: From the summit, marked by the large metallic cross, you'll be treated to expansive vistas. You can see the Ebro valley and a wide panorama including the Montes de Campoo, Castro Valnera, Humión (the highest peak in the Obarenes), Gorbeia, Toloño, and the Sierra de Cantabria, with Aralar and Aizkorri visible in the distance. Buzzards are often seen circling overhead.

faqs.question: Are there any amenities or points of interest along the way besides the main highlights?
faqs.answer: Beyond the main highlights like the Monasterio de Herrera and the Cruz de Motrico, you'll find amenities at the San Juan del Monte starting point, including parking and a fountain. Along the route, there's also a restored old shepherd's hut and plaques identifying local vegetation, adding to the trail's charm.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular route?
faqs.answer: For jogging, this 14.2 km route with over 500 meters of elevation gain is a significant workout and is estimated to take around 1 hour and 57 minutes. If you're hiking at a moderate pace, expect it to take closer to 3-4 hours, especially considering the challenging sections.
